questions.prompt: What's one major economic advantage of Cloudflare Pages vs Vercel/Netlify for bandwidth-heavy sites?

choices.text: Free egress. Cloudflare doesn't bill bandwidth for Pages or R2; Vercel and Netlify do (with allowances, then per-GB). For video-heavy or download-heavy sites, this can mean thousands per month in savings.

questions.explanation: Free egress is Cloudflare's structural advantage on bandwidth-heavy workloads. For traffic-light sites the difference is marginal; for media/video/file-heavy sites, it can be a huge cost difference. Bandwidth bills are why teams sometimes migrate to Pages from competitors.

questions.prompt: You have a Next.js app heavily using ISR and specific Node APIs that don't fit Edge Runtime. Should you ship on Pages or Vercel?

choices.text: Vercel has the deepest Next.js integration and supports the full Node runtime, including ISR and Node-specific APIs. Pages supports Next.js via Edge Runtime, which has gaps for some advanced features. For pure Next.js with advanced features, Vercel often wins. Hybrid: keep Cloudflare for edge concerns (DNS, security, caching in front), Vercel for the Next.js app.

questions.explanation: Pick by framework fit. Pages is excellent for static, Jamstack, and Next.js Edge Runtime workloads — but full Next.js Node runtime with all features is Vercel's strength. Hybrid (Cloudflare in front + Vercel app) is the canonical pattern when each excels at different parts.
